title: The Organism Seen From the Whole — Steiner Reading Goethe's Morphology
author: Rudolf Steiner / Goethe

body.text: Steiner's reading of Goethe begins with a distinction. Inorganic phenomena can be understood by external relation — one body strikes another, one condition produces another. The organism requires a different eye. A living thing is not assembled from parts. Its parts are expressions of a whole.
The great significance of Goethe's morphological works rests on the fact that in them he was the first to extend the idea of the whole to the study of living organisms.

Steiner calls this a scientific deed because it gives method, result. Goethe found a way to study life without reducing it to mechanism. The living being is grasped by seeing how the particular part proceeds from the whole. The imagination does not invent a hidden whole. It learns to perceive the whole through the transformations of the parts.

body.caption: The whole expressing itself through organs — metamorphosis as visible continuity.

body.text: Again the matter is otherwise for Goethe. They complement one another. Goethe's ideas provide the foundation for the other view.

The key phrase is 'inner, ideal relatedness.' Goethe deepens outer similarity into inward kinship. Organs resemble each other because they are inwardly related. The visible rests on an invisible law. The first rendering shows separate plant-parts. The Goethean rendering shows one formative idea moving through them.
Goethe is the Copernicus and Kepler of the organic world.

This is Steiner's strongest image. Before Kepler, people saw the heavens. After Kepler, they saw lawful motion. Before Goethe, people saw plants and animals. After Goethe, they could begin to see metamorphosis. The comparison frames imagination as scientific — a discovery of law where mechanical thinking cannot reach.

body.caption: Kepler of the organic world — lawful motion seen in living form.

body.text: The essential being of the particular itself presses toward the general — the typus.

Every particular being asks to be understood through more than its particularity. A leaf is this leaf with this edge and colour. But it presses toward the typus — the ideal-general that makes it a leaf within a living sequence. The Goethean eye holds both: the particular as local appearance of a general formative law.

This is the eye that sees livingly. Do not dissolve the particular into abstraction or trap it as isolated fact. Hold both together. The organism is only known when both its here-ness and its lawfulness are perceived in a single act.
